core_intuitions: The residual stream is a width-n_embd highway with additive on-ramps: every block reads from it and adds back (attention adds a cross-token term, MLP adds a per-token term); nothing overwrites, everything accumulates.
Attention mixes information ACROSS tokens; the MLP processes EACH token independently. These are the two complementary operations every block performs.

The parameter budget: per block 12 n_embd^2, split as attention 4 n_embd^2 and MLP 8 n_embd^2 (MLP holds twice attention); body ~= 12 n_layer n_embd^2; the tied embedding (V x n_embd) is a rounding error for tiny vocab but dominates for large (BPE) vocab.

A GPT predicts the next token at EVERY position simultaneously: targets are inputs shifted by one, the causal mask stops position t from seeing token t+1, so one (B,T) batch yields B*T next-token training signals and an untrained loss of ~ln V.

Scaled residual init: because each of the 2*n_layer sublayers adds variance to the stream, shrinking the output projections by 1/sqrt(2 n_layer) keeps the residual stream's scale roughly constant with depth.

Decoding knobs reshape the same next-token distribution: temperature scales it (tau->0 greedy, tau->inf uniform), top-k keeps a fixed count, top-p keeps an adaptive nucleus sized by the model's confidence.

existing.name: buildgpt-data-flow-stages
existing.issue: Excellent, correct, and theme-safe in both light and dark: it nails the residual-stream-as-highway intuition, the two additive on-ramps per block, weight tying, and the softmax-to-distribution tail. The one blemish is mild crowding on the right: the descriptor labels 'mixes ACROSS tokens' and 'processes EACH token' render larger than their small ln_1/ln_2 boxes and overlap the box right edge, so the ACROSS/EACH contrast (a core intuition) reads slightly muddled.
existing.improvement: Move the two descriptor phrases so they no longer collide with the ln_1/ln_2 boxes: either right-align them cleanly to the right of the boxes with a small gap, or place them as a sub-caption directly beneath each box, and keep them at a genuinely smaller size than the box titles. No structural change otherwise; the diagram is otherwise a keep.

new_figures.name: gpt-parameter-budget
new_figures.concept: Where the parameters of a GPT actually live: the per-block 4d^2 (attention) vs 8d^2 (MLP) split, the 12 n_layer n_embd^2 body formula, and the tied-embedding crossover between tiny and large vocabularies.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter hammers this with a full worked example and a Key Takeaway, and calls the 12 n_layer n_embd^2 formula 'worth memorizing.' The two facts readers must internalize are geometric proportions -- MLP is twice attention, and the embedding is negligible for small vocab but dominant for large vocab -- which a proportional bar makes obvious in one glance and prose never quite does.
new_figures.placement: After the admonition 'Worked example: counting the parameters of our default GPT' in the section 'The Full GPT Module'.
new_figures.brief: A two-panel infographic, both panels theme-safe (use two distinct accent hues for attention vs MLP, a neutral fill for embeddings; label everything in units of d^2 = n_embd^2 rather than fake totals). PANEL A -- 'Inside one block': a single horizontal proportional bar of total width 12 d^2. Left segment = 4 d^2 labelled 'Attention' and subdivided/annotated 'c_attn 3d^2 + c_proj d^2'; right segment = 8 d^2 labelled 'MLP', subdivided/annotated 'c_fc 4d^2 + c_proj 4d^2'. A bracket or callout under the two segments reads 'MLP holds 2x attention's parameters'. A caption tag: 'x n_layer blocks  ->  body ~= 12 n_layer n_embd^2'. PANEL B -- 'Where the embedding sits (vocab crossover)': two stacked-bar mini-charts side by side. Left chart 'tiny vocab (char-level, V=65)': a tall bar = the transformer body (12 n_layer d^2) with a barely-visible sliver on top = tied embedding (V*d), annotated 'embedding = rounding error'. Right chart 'large vocab (BPE, V~50k)': the SAME body bar height, but now the embedding block is comparable-to-larger than the whole body, annotated 'tied embedding now dominates'. On both, tag the lm_head with a small 'shared w/ wte -> +0 params' note to show tying costs nothing. Keep all numbers illustrative and derived only (V=65, V=50k, the 4/8/12 d^2 multiples); invent no benchmark figures. Ensure both panels fit inside the viewBox with clear left-to-right reading order and no label overlap. The single takeaway a reader should leave with: MLP > attention per block, and embeddings only matter when the vocabulary is big.

new_figures.name: next-token-training-signal
new_figures.concept: One sequence yields T next-token predictions at once: targets are inputs shifted by one, the causal mask forbids seeing the future, and an untrained model's loss is ~ln V.
new_figures.rationale: This is THE mechanic that makes language-model training work, and the chapter dwells on it repeatedly (the anatomy 'predict at every position', the get_batch shift-by-one, the ln V sanity check, and the two 'too good to be true' pitfalls). Readers must SEE the x/y offset alignment and the triangular mask together to grasp why one forward pass gives B*T signals and why a leaked mask or bad shift silently drives loss toward zero -- an alignment diagram conveys this instantly where prose forces the reader to simulate it mentally.
new_figures.placement: After the get_batch code block and the 'shift by one' paragraph in the section 'Tokenizing and batching'.
new_figures.brief: A single static alignment infographic in three linked zones, theme-safe (neutral token cells, one accent for the 'next-token' target arrows, a muted fill for masked-out cells). ZONE 1 (top) -- two aligned rows of ~6 token cells using a short illustrative char string. Row x (inputs): cells like [T][o][ ][b][e][ ]. Row y (targets): the SAME string shifted left by one: [o][ ][b][e][ ][?]. Draw a diagonal arrow from each x[t] region up to y[t] labelled once as 'y[t] = x[t+1]  (targets = inputs shifted left by 1)'. ZONE 2 (middle-left) -- a small lower-triangular causal-mask grid (about 5x5): filled cells on/below the diagonal, muted cells above, with a one-line annotation 'position t may attend to 1..t, never t+1'. ZONE 3 (middle-right / bottom) -- show the model emitting a tiny next-token bar-distribution at EVERY position simultaneously (a row of T little distributions), with a brace labelled 'T predictions from ONE forward pass  ->  loss = mean cross-entropy over all B*T positions'. Add a small side note near the distributions: 'untrained: each ~ uniform  ->  loss ~= ln V'. Finally, a compact two-line 'watch out' callout: 'y not shifted  ->  model just copies input (loss -> 0)' and 'mask leaks  ->  model sees the answer (loss -> 0)'. Keep the string, mask size, and any counts illustrative and small; no invented metrics. Everything must fit the viewBox with an unambiguous top-to-bottom, left-to-right reading order.

new_figures.name: sampling-temperature-topk-topp
new_figures.concept: The three decoding knobs reshape one next-token distribution differently: temperature scales it, top-k keeps a fixed count, top-p keeps an adaptive nucleus.
new_figures.rationale: The Sampling section is a core deliverable and its worked example is purely numeric; readers need to SEE that temperature reshapes the ENTIRE distribution (not just the top) while top-k and top-p TRUNCATE it in fundamentally different ways -- fixed-count vs confidence-adaptive. The adaptivity of top-p (many tokens when uncertain, few when confident) is exactly what the Interview Corner leans on to explain degenerate repetition, and it is nearly impossible to feel from prose alone.
new_figures.placement: In the section 'What the knobs actually do' (under 'Sampling: Turning Logits Into Text'), after the temperature/top-k/top-p paragraphs.
new_figures.brief: A two-panel static infographic built on ONE shared base distribution (reuse the chapter's illustrative logits z=[3.0,1.0,0.5,-1.0] plus a few tiny tail bars; these are the text's own example numbers, not invented benchmarks). Theme-safe: neutral bars, one accent for 'kept' mass, muted grey for 'chopped' mass. PANEL A -- 'Temperature reshapes everything': three small bar charts of the same 4+ candidates side by side, labelled tau=0.5 (sharpened: one dominant bar, tail near zero), tau=1.0 (the base distribution), tau=2.0 (flattened: the rare tail bar visibly rises). A spanning label reads 'tau -> 0 : greedy/deterministic    tau -> inf : uniform'. PANEL B -- 'Top-k vs top-p truncate differently': two mini bar charts of the base distribution. Left 'top-k (fixed count)': keep the k highest bars in accent, grey out the rest, with a hard vertical cut line labelled 'k = 2 (always the same count)'. Right 'top-p (nucleus, adaptive)': shade a cumulative fill sweeping left-to-right until it reaches p, cut there, label 'p = 0.9 (cut where cumulative prob reaches p)'. Beneath top-p, add a small contrast pair of two tiny distributions -- one 'confident' (nucleus keeps few tokens) and one 'uncertain' (nucleus keeps many) -- to make the adaptivity legible, annotated 'top-p keeps FEW when confident, MANY when unsure'. Add a faint 'then renormalize' tag on the kept mass. All bars, k, and p are illustrative; fit everything in the viewBox with clear panel separation and no overlapping labels.

summary: One-figure chapter for a 923-line capstone; the existing data-flow diagram is strong (keep, minor label-crowding refine). Three foundational static figures fill the unserved core intuitions the chapter hammers with worked examples: the parameter budget (4d^2 vs 8d^2 vs embedding crossover), the next-token training signal (shift-by-one + causal mask -> B*T signals + ln V), and the decoding knobs (temperature vs top-k vs top-p reshaping). All static; no animation needed.
